---
title: "Lab 7"
subtitle: "Categorical A/B Forensics: When \"p < 0.05\" Is a Trap"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Objective

In **Lab 6** you learned that a clean $t$-test verdict is not enough, because
the $t$-test only ever looks at the **mean**. **Lab 7 moves to the other half
of product data — counts in categories — and the lesson sharpens: a single
"the chi-square is significant, ship it" line is, more often than not, a
trap.**

The GreenBowl experimentation platform A/B-tested a new **menu layout**. For
every order it logged the **basket tier** the user landed in — *Economy /
Standard / Large / Premium / VIP* — and the **city** the order came from. The
automated report prints a tidy chi-square line claiming the redesign *changed
the basket mix*. Your job is to decide whether you can **believe** it.

The lecture gave you a toolkit where everything reduces to one idea — **compare
observed counts $O$ with the counts $E$ expected under a hypothesis**,
$\tau = \sum (O-E)^2/E$ — and several guardrails that the headline number
quietly skips:

- **Goodness-of-fit as an SRM check** — was the **randomization** itself
  honest (did the arms arrive in the intended 50/50 split)?
- **The expected-count rule ($E \ge 5$)** — is the table even *dense enough*
  for the chi-square approximation to be valid?
- **Chi-square homogeneity + standardized residuals** — did the distribution
  move, and *where*?
- **Cramér's V** — on big $n$ everything is "significant"; is the effect
  actually **strong**?
- **Yates / Fisher** — on a tiny pilot, does the exact test agree with the
  approximation?
- **Test of independence** — is an apparent win really driven by a
  **confounding covariate** (the city)?
- **Monte Carlo** — is your own guardrail calibrated at *your* sample size?

The twist, as in Labs 4–6: **you do not get to see what is wrong.** Your
experiment is pulled live from `greenbowl_chi.py`, keyed to your student id.
Some students received a genuine, business-meaningful shift; some a difference
that is statistically "significant" yet microscopic; some a **broken split**;
some a **micro-pilot** too small for the chi-square approximation; some a
**rare category** that quietly invalidates the test; and some an apparent win
that is really a **city confound**. **The only honest way to find out which one
you hold is to run the diagnostics.**

You will:

1. Pull your experiment and read the platform's **headline chi-square verdict**
   — the one you are about to put on trial.
2. Run the **SRM guardrail** (a goodness-of-fit test of the delivered split
   against the intended 50/50) — the categorical A/A.
3. Enforce the **expected-count rule** and build a `safe_homogeneity` tool that
   refuses to trust a sparse table.
4. Run the **homogeneity test**, read the **standardized residuals**, and pair
   the $p$-value with **Cramér's V** to separate "significant" from "strong".
5. Run the **independence check** (`arm × city`) and, if your covariate is
   imbalanced, **stratify** to expose a confound.
6. **Validate your guardrail with Monte Carlo** — its FPR, the minimum $n$, and
   its power for *your* kind of effect.
7. Deliver a one-page **forensic verdict** a non-statistician PM can act on.

# General Requirements

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## The fixed business context (same for everyone)

- **Unit:** one order.
- **Metric:** the **basket tier** — `Economy`, `Standard`, `Large`,
  `Premium`, `VIP` (ordered from cheapest to most expensive). The business
  cares about users moving **up** into `Large`/`Premium`/`VIP`.
- **Covariate:** the **city** (`Kyiv`, `Lviv`, `Odesa`, `Kharkiv`), logged for
  every order.
- **Arms:** `control` = old menu layout, `test` = new layout.
- **Intended split:** **50 / 50**. Whether it was *delivered* 50/50 is
  something you must check.
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Everyone's experiment is different — on purpose

Your experiment is unique to your id. It might be a clean win, a significant-
but-trivial change, a broken randomization (SRM), a micro-pilot too small to
trust, a table with a rare category that breaks the approximation, or an
apparent win driven by a city confound. **There is no single "right answer"
shared across the class** — there is only the diagnosis your diagnostics
support. Copying another student's conclusion will be obviously wrong for your
experiment.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Report Format

Submit the report in **one** of the following formats:

- **Quarto → PDF** (`.qmd` → `.pdf`)
- **Quarto → HTML** (`.qmd` → `.html`, hosted, e.g. on
  [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com/),
  [Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/))
- **Jupyter Notebook** (`.ipynb`)
- **Google Colab** (share the link with viewing permissions)

The report must contain both code **and** narrative. Every section begins with
a written explanation, not code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## What you build vs. what you use ready-made

The thing you **build** today is the **categorical guardrail toolkit** —
`srm_check`, `homogeneity_test` → `homogeneity_report` → `safe_homogeneity`,
and the Monte-Carlo validators — grown step by step from Practice 7, each
version **reusing** the previous one. The **criteria** themselves you take
ready-made: `scipy.stats.chisquare`, `scipy.stats.chi2_contingency`,
`scipy.stats.fisher_exact`, and
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint` for the Wilson interval. You
may **not** replace your validators with a library power/AA function — the
simulation loop and the confidence-interval verdict must be **your own code**.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Honour rule on the data platform

`greenbowl_chi.py` contains the hidden truth in its source. **Do not read past
the `# === instructor section ===` line, and do not call `_reveal`.** The point
of the lab is to reach the diagnosis *by running the diagnostics*, not by
reading the answer. Any report that names the hidden pathology from the source
instead of demonstrating it with the toolkit scores **zero** for
interpretation.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Deadline

📅 **July 1, 2026, 23:59**
:::

# Tasks

## Get Your Experiment {#sec-data}

Download `greenbowl_chi.py` (provided with the lab) into your working folder
and load **your** experiment. Use **one** id consistently — your KSE email is
recommended.

```{python}
#| label: load
#| eval: false

from greenbowl_chi import (load_my_experiment, BASKETS, CITIES,
                           INTENDED_SPLIT, ALPHA)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import chisquare, chi2_contingency, fisher_exact
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint

STUDENT_ID = "your.email@kse.org.ua"   # <-- put YOUR id here
exp = load_my_experiment(STUDENT_ID)

print(exp)                                  # arm sizes + intended split
print(exp.data.head())                      # one row per order: arm, basket, city
table = exp.table()                         # 2 x 5 counts: rows control/test
print("rows = [control, test], cols =", BASKETS)
print(table)
```

::: {.border}
1. State your `STUDENT_ID`. Report $n_{\text{control}}$, $n_{\text{test}}$, and
   the $2\times 5$ **basket table** (rows `control`/`test`, columns
   `BASKETS`).
2. Plot the basket distribution of each arm as **shares** (grouped bars), so
   the two arms are comparable despite any size difference. In one sentence,
   describe what you see.
3. Run the platform's **headline test** — a naive
   `chi2_contingency(table)` over the full $2\times 5$ table — and write down
   the "ship / don't ship" line it would print. **This is the verdict you are
   about to put on trial** — do not believe it yet.
4. Confirm in one sentence that you understand the experiment's true nature is
   **hidden**, and your task is to *diagnose* it with the categorical toolkit.
:::

## The SRM Guardrail: Was the Split Honest? (`srm_check`) {#sec-srm}

Before believing **any** effect, check that the experiment was allocated the
way it was designed. A **Sample-Ratio Mismatch (SRM)** — the delivered arm
sizes drifting from the intended 50/50 — is the categorical A/B world's
equivalent of a broken randomization: if the split is biased, *who* is in each
arm is biased too, and every downstream comparison is contaminated. The right
tool is a **goodness-of-fit** test of the two arm counts against the intended
ratio.

::: {.border}
1. Implement **Version 1** of your toolkit,
   `srm_check(n_control, n_test, intended=INTENDED_SPLIT, alpha=0.05)`, using
   `scipy.stats.chisquare` with `f_exp` set to the intended split, returning
   the $\chi^2$, the $p$-value, and a human-readable verdict. (This is a $k=2$
   goodness-of-fit test — recall the lecture's "$<10$ for a $2\times1$ table"
   note on expected counts.)
2. Run it on **your** arm sizes. State plainly: **does the split pass or fail
   SRM**, and by how many percentage points the realized ratio differs from
   50/50.
3. **Interpret as a guardrail.** A rejection here means the assignment / logging
   is broken — *not* that the feature did anything. Explain, in one sentence,
   why a difference you later see in the basket mix **cannot** be attributed to
   the layout if the SRM check fails.
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Stop-the-line rule
If the **SRM check fails**, the basket comparison is **void** until the
allocation is fixed — a "win" on a biased split is most likely **differential
dropout** (e.g. low-spenders silently missing from one arm), not a real effect.
If it fails, say so plainly: the rest of your analysis becomes "here is the
bug", not "here is the effect".
:::

## Expected-Count Hygiene (`safe_homogeneity`) {#sec-expected}

The chi-square test relies on a normal approximation (the CLT) that **breaks
for rare categories**. The rule: every **expected** count $E_{ij} \ge 5$
(and $\ge 10$ for a $2\times 2$). A table can *look* fine — non-zero
everywhere — yet have an expected count below 5 hiding in a rare column, and
then the $p$-value is not to be trusted.

::: {.border}
1. Implement **Version 2**, `homogeneity_test(table, alpha=0.05)`, using
   `scipy.stats.chi2_contingency`, returning $\chi^2$, the degrees of freedom,
   the $p$-value, **`min_expected`** (the smallest expected count), and a
   verdict (mirror Practice 7's `assoc_test`).
2. Run `homogeneity_test` on your full $2\times 5$ table and report
   `min_expected`. **Is the rule satisfied?** If `min_expected < 5` (look at
   the rare `VIP` tier especially), **merge** `VIP` into `Premium` and re-run.
   Did the verdict change? (If your table is so small that `chi2_contingency`
   cannot even run — an all-zero column — that itself is a verdict: the table
   is too sparse for this test.)
3. You will turn this guard into a reusable tool (`safe_homogeneity`) in
   @sec-report, once the report version exists for it to fall back on.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## The rule is about *expected*, not *observed*
A category can have a healthy observed count yet a tiny expected one, or vice
versa. Always read `min_expected` from `chi2_contingency`, not the zeros you
happen to see in the table.
:::

## Did the Mix Move, and Is It Strong? (`homogeneity_report`) {#sec-report}

Now the headline question — but answered honestly. The chi-square homogeneity
test says **whether** the basket distribution differs between arms; the
**standardized residuals** say **where**; and **Cramér's V** says **how
strongly** — which the $p$-value, on a large sample, cannot.

::: {.border}
1. Implement **Version 3**, `homogeneity_report(table, alpha=0.05)`, that
   **reuses** `homogeneity_test` (Version 2) and adds **Cramér's V**,
   $V = \sqrt{\chi^2 / (n\,(\min(m,k)-1))}$, plus a verbal strength label
   (`practically none` $<0.1$, `weak` $<0.2$, `moderate` $<0.4$, else
   `strong`).
2. Implement **Version 4**, `safe_homogeneity(table, alpha=0.05)`, your
   all-in-one entry point, that **reuses** `homogeneity_report` and adds a
   guard: if the table is $2\times 2$ **and** `min_expected < 10` (the
   stricter $2\times 2$ threshold from the lecture), switch to `fisher_exact`;
   otherwise, if any `min_expected < 5`, **refuse to trust the raw chi-square**
   and flag that a category must be merged (mirror Practice 7's `safe_assoc`).
   Use `safe_homogeneity` from here on.
3. Run it on the table you judged **valid** in @sec-expected (raw or merged).
   Report $p$, $V$, and the strength label.
4. **If your full table was too sparse for a valid 5-tier test** (every
   $E < 5$, or `chi2_contingency` would not even run on an all-zero column —
   the micro-pilot case), do **not** report a 5-tier verdict. Collapse to the
   business-critical $2\times 2$ — `Premium`+`VIP` ("upmarket") vs the rest —
   and run `safe_homogeneity` on it: with small expected counts it will route
   to **Fisher's exact test**. Report the exact $p$ and treat *that* as your
   verdict for this experiment.
5. Plot the **standardized residuals** $(O-E)/\sqrt{E}$ as a heatmap
   (`imshow`, diverging colormap, annotated). Read it in business terms: *which
   basket tiers gained or lost in the test arm?* Is the movement **upward**
   (toward `Premium`/`VIP`) or not?
6. **Significant ≠ strong.** State explicitly: if your $n$ is very large and
   $p \approx 0$ but $V$ is tiny, the change is **real but trivial** — quote
   the actual shift in the `Premium`+`Large`+`VIP` **share** in percentage
   points and judge whether it clears a business threshold. Conversely, a
   non-trivial $V$ with an upward residual pattern is a genuine candidate to
   ship.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Cramér's V is biased **upward** on small samples
$V$ is inflated when $n$ is small. Under $H_0$,
$\mathbb{E}[V^2] = \dfrac{(m-1)(k-1)}{n\,(\min(m,k)-1)}$, so
$$
\mathbb{E}[V] \lesssim \sqrt{\frac{(m-1)(k-1)}{n\,(\min(m,k)-1)}}
\;\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}\; 0 .
$$
The bias **shrinks as $n$ grows** — that is the whole point. For a $2\times 5$
table at $n=30$ this floor is $\sqrt{4/30}\approx 0.37$, which is exactly why a
15-vs-15 pilot shows $V\approx 0.3$ with **no** real effect. Do **not** read
that as a "moderate" association: a large $V$ only means something at a healthy
$n$; at tiny $n$, trust the exact-test $p$-value, not $V$. (For an honest
number, use the **bias-corrected** Cramér's V, e.g. Bergsma's.)
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Research hook
Two of the hidden worlds both **reject** the homogeneity test — but one is a
real win and the other is a microscopic difference inflated by an enormous
sample. The chi-square $p$-value **cannot** tell them apart. Cramér's V and the
percentage-point shift can. This is exactly the big-$n$ trap from the lecture.
:::

## The Confound Check: Is the "Win" Really the City? {#sec-confound}

Homogeneity assumes the arms differ **only** by the treatment. But if the split
accidentally over-sampled one **city** in the test arm — and that city has
different basket tastes — the pooled table can show a "shift" that is pure
**covariate imbalance**, not the layout. This is a **test of independence**
question, and the fix is to **stratify**.

::: {.border}
1. **Check the covariate balance.** Build the `arm × city` table
   (`exp.table(by="arm_city")`) and run `homogeneity_test` on it. Is the
   **city distribution independent of the arm**, or did the test arm
   over-/under-sample some city?
2. **If `arm × city` is balanced:** confirm in one sentence that the city is
   not confounding your basket comparison, and your @sec-report verdict stands.
3. **If `arm × city` is imbalanced:** the pooled basket result is **suspect**.
   **Stratify** — run `homogeneity_test` on the basket table *within each city*
   (`exp.table(by="city")`) and compare. If the within-city tables **stop
   rejecting** while the pooled one rejected, the apparent effect is a **city
   confound** (a Simpson-style reversal): report the layout as **not** the
   cause, and recommend fixing the split / controlling for city. Read the four
   within-city $p$-values with **multiplicity** in mind (a single $p$ just
   under $0.05$ across four cities is expected by chance — think Bonferroni).
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Research hook
This is the part you must *discover*, not assume. Most ids have a balanced
`arm × city`; some do not. "I checked `arm × city`, it is independent, so city
is not confounding the result" is a real finding — and skipping the check when
the covariate *is* imbalanced is a real mistake that ships a non-effect.
:::

## Validate Your Guardrail with Monte Carlo {#sec-mc}

Your homogeneity test is itself a **criterion**, so validate it as in Practice
7 / Lab 5: does it keep the promised 5% false positives at **your** table
shape and sample size, and **from what $n$** can it detect an effect like
yours? You set the truth, so you always know the right answer.

::: {.border}
1. Implement `homogeneity_fpr(probs, n_per_arm, n_exps=2000, alpha=0.05,
   seed=...)` that runs an **A/A** ($H_0$ true: **both** arms drawn from the
   same `probs` with `np.random.default_rng(...).multinomial`) and returns the
   FPR **with** a 95% **Wilson** CI (`proportion_confint(method="wilson")`).
2. **Calibration.** Run it for the GreenBowl base mix at a small, a medium and
   a large $n$ (e.g. 30 / 200 / 2000). Report each FPR with its CI and whether
   $\alpha = 0.05$ is inside. **Then make a category rare** (push `VIP` toward
   $\approx 0.01$) at small $n$ and show the FPR **leave** $\alpha$ — the
   Monte-Carlo proof of the $E \ge 5$ rule from @sec-expected. Plot FPR (with
   the Wilson band) vs. $n$ with a dashed line at $0.05$.
3. **Power for *your* effect.** Implement
   `homogeneity_power(p_control, p_test, n_per_arm, ...)` and measure the power
   to detect an effect **like the one in your experiment** (the basket shift,
   or — if your world is the trivial / null one — a deliberately small shift,
   to show how much $n$ a tiny effect needs). How large an $n$ per arm would
   you need for power $\ge 0.8$?
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Match the validation to *your* diagnosis
Validate the criterion your verdict actually rests on:

- **Micro-pilot (the 15-vs-15 case):** your decision hinges on a small
  $2\times 2$, so validate **that** — run the A/A FPR of the $2\times 2$
  chi-square **with and without Yates' correction** at small $n$ (reuse the
  Practice-7 `chi2_rejection_rate` idea), and show that without correction it
  **over-rejects** while Fisher / Yates holds $\alpha$. That over-rejection is
  *why* you trusted Fisher over the naive chi-square.
- **Rare-category world:** validate the $E \ge 5$ rule directly (step 2) and
  show that **merging** restores an honest FPR.
- **Big-$n$ trap (trivial world):** the criterion is calibrated fine — the
  point is **power**, not FPR. Show that at your $n$ even a microscopic effect
  is detected with power $\approx 1$, which is exactly why $p$ is uninformative
  about strength.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Why this matters
Whatever you conclude rests on the criterion being trustworthy at *your* table
shape and size. "Conservative" (FPR below $\alpha$) is **safe but
underpowered**; "invalid" (FPR above $\alpha$, e.g. from a rare cell) is **not**
— a rejection from an invalid test is worth nothing. Keep that distinction from
Lab 5.
:::

## Final Forensic Verdict {#sec-final}

::: {.border}
Write a **single-page memo** (≤ 400 words) to a **non-technical** GreenBowl PM
who has only seen the platform's headline chi-square line. It must contain:

1. The **headline verdict** (what the automated report claimed) and your
   **one-line forensic verdict** — one of: a real, business-meaningful basket
   shift (**ship**); a **significant-but-trivial** change (**don't ship**); a
   **broken split / SRM** (**fix the splitter**, result void); an
   **underpowered micro-pilot** (**collect more**); a table invalidated by a
   **rare category** (**fix the test**, no effect); or an apparent win that is
   a **city confound** (**don't trust it**, control for city).
2. The **evidence** that forces it, quoted as numbers, not adjectives: the SRM
   check, `min_expected` and any merge, the homogeneity $p$ **and** Cramér's V,
   the residual pattern, the `arm × city` independence check (with stratified
   results if relevant), and the Monte-Carlo calibration/power at your $n$.
3. A concrete **recommendation**: ship / don't ship / fix the splitter / merge
   and re-test / collect more / control for city — whichever your diagnosis
   demands.
4. One honest **caveat** of your own audit (e.g. chi-square tells you *whether*,
   not whether it is business-significant; a single stratified $p$ near $0.05$
   across four cities is multiplicity, not signal; simulation noise at the
   `n_exps` you used).

It must be readable by someone who has never heard the words "chi-square" or
"$p$-value". You are graded on whether the PM could act on it without
follow-up questions.
:::

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points |
|---|:---:|
| Correctness of methodology | 3 |
| Interpretation of results | 3 |
| Code quality and organization | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Grading Details

**Correctness of methodology (3 pts):**

- `srm_check` and `homogeneity_test`/`homogeneity_report`/`safe_homogeneity`
  are correct, built step by step (each version reusing the previous), and
  `min_expected` / the merge rule is enforced (1 pt).
- The SRM guardrail, the homogeneity test with residuals + Cramér's V, and the
  `arm × city` independence/stratification check are all run correctly with the
  right tables and df (1 pt).
- The Monte-Carlo validation returns the FPR/power **with** a Wilson CI from a
  correct simulation loop, and is read for calibration vs. power (1 pt).

**Interpretation of results (3 pts):**

- @sec-srm and @sec-expected correctly gate the analysis (a failed SRM or a
  sub-5 expected count is treated as disqualifying, not ignored).
- @sec-report separates **significant** from **strong** (Cramér's V + the
  percentage-point shift), and @sec-confound handles the city confound
  correctly when present.
- The memo's forensic verdict **matches the evidence** and overturns or
  confirms the headline chi-square for the right reason.

**Code quality and organization (2 pts):**

- `srm_check`, the homogeneity tools, and the Monte-Carlo validators are single
  reusable functions used across all sections (built step by step, not
  re-implemented inline).
- Plots (share bars, residual heatmap, the FPR-vs-$n$ curve) have axis labels,
  titles, legends, and a dashed $\alpha$ line where relevant.
- Narrative precedes every code block; each section reads as an argument, not a
  dump of outputs.
:::

# Defense {.unnumbered}

During the defense (5–7 minutes) you will be asked *"What if?"* questions that
probe whether you understand **why** each piece exists, for example:

- *"Your chi-square $p$ is below $10^{-20}$. Does that mean the redesign had a
  big effect? What single number settles it — the $p$-value or something
  else?"*
- *"Your arms came in 4000 vs 3700. Why does that one fact make the basket
  'win' untrustworthy, no matter how small the homogeneity $p$ is?"*
- *"Your `VIP` column has an expected count of 3. Show me — live — what you do
  about it and why a $p$-value off the raw table would be misleading."*
- *"Your pooled table rejects but the within-city tables don't. Explain to the
  PM, in one sentence, what actually happened."*
- *"On 15 vs 15 orders the naive chi-square said 'significant' but Fisher said
  no. Which do you believe, and what does your Monte-Carlo FPR at $n=15$ tell
  you about the naive test?"*
- *"Your Cramér's V is $0.01$ with $p \approx 0$. Is that a contradiction? What
  do you tell the PM?"*

You may also be asked to **change one thing live** (merge a category, swap the
pooled table for a stratified one, shrink $n$ in the Monte-Carlo, toggle Yates'
correction) and explain the resulting shift.
